text.title: Tag design: naming, attributes, nesting
text.summary: Concrete rules for choosing tag names, when to use attributes, and how to nest hierarchies.

rule.statement: Tag names use lowercase ASCII, hyphenated for multi-word concepts, and describe content role.
rule.rationale: Consistent casing avoids parser ambiguity. Descriptive names let the agent route policy by tag without reading content.

example.note: CamelCase, generic names, or numbered tags carry no role signal.

rule.statement: Use attributes for fixed metadata (id, type, lang, path); use child elements for content the agent will read or quote.
rule.rationale: Attributes are cheap to query and rare to misparse; child text is what the agent renders or quotes back.

example.note: Metadata wrapped as elements bloats the tree and hides what the agent should read.

rule.statement: Nest only when the relationship is naturally hierarchical: container/items, parent-rule/sub-clauses, example/code+note.
rule.rationale: Anthropic's own pattern: documents → document → document_content + source. Flat lists with descriptive tags beat deep trees.

example.note: Five levels of generic wrappers. Agent sees noise.

section.title: Code blocks

rule.statement: Wrap source code in `<code lang="...">` with a CDATA section. Never let `<`, `>`, or `&` leak into element content.
rule.rationale: CDATA preserves code verbatim and prevents the parser from misreading code as XML. The lang attribute lets the agent pick the right interpretation (syntax, formatter, runner).

section.reference: Skeleton with a CDATA-wrapped code example.

rule.statement: Link to other texts and templates with `<reference path="...">` (one-line purpose) or `<see-also><link path="...">...</link></see-also>` (related reading).
rule.rationale: Two distinct relations: a reference is consumed (template, script, schema); a see-also is optional context. Different tags let the agent prioritize.
